hypotheses: Hypothesis 1: Bimodal rating distributions are perceived as more indicative of self-expression.
Hypothesis 2: Products with polarized ratings for self-expressive attributes are more likely to be perceived as self-expressive.
Hypothesis 3: Products with bimodal distributions will be preferred by those with high self-expression needs, arising from dispositional or contextual factors.
Hypothesis 4: For products polarized among in-group members, preference will vary based on whether individual or group identity expression needs are greater.
claim.statement: The research examines how polarized (i.e., bimodal) rating distributions affect perceptions of product self-expressiveness, particularly for attributes related to self-expression (e.g., style) rather than functional attributes (e.g., quality).

data.wave_or_subset: Study 1a and 1b, Study 2, Study 3, Study 4, Study 5
data.notes: Participants were paid $0.80 each. Experiments conducted online.

method.description: Participants were shown different product rating distributions and asked to evaluate products based on these distributions in varied contexts of self-expression.
steps: Assign participants to study conditions involving bimodal or unimodal rating distributions.
Present product evaluations within different contextual scenarios (e.g., self-expressive vs. functional).
Collect responses using scales measuring self-expressiveness, desirability, and willingness to pay.
Analyze data to assess influence of rating type on perceived self-expression and product desirability.
method.control_variables: Self-concept clarity, context of use (self-expressive vs. functional)

results.summary: Bimodal rating distributions are perceived as more self-expressive and increase desirability under self-expression contexts.

metadata.original_paper_title: Polarizing Products: The Impact of Bimodal Rating Distributions on Consumer Preferences
